title: Learned Features: The Deep Learning Shift
concept: Instead of a human hand-designing every filter, a neural network can learn its own filter values directly from data — the same weight-adjusting idea behind the perceptron and backpropagation, now applied to image filters.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What's the core problem with hand-crafted filter pipelines?

options.text: They don't generalize well — new conditions usually need a human to redesign them by hand
They run too slowly to be used in real products
They only work on images smaller than 100x100 pixels

exercises.explain: Right — hand-tuning doesn't scale. Today: what if the filters designed themselves?

cards.title: What if the filter designed itself?
cards.body: Every classical filter — blur, Sobel, Canny — has a human-chosen set of numbers behind it. What if, instead, those numbers weren't chosen by a person at all, but discovered automatically by looking at thousands of examples?

cards.title: What are learned features?
cards.body: Learned features are filters whose numeric values are discovered automatically by a neural network during training, instead of being hand-designed by an engineer. The network starts with random numbers and adjusts them until the filter extracts whatever pattern actually turns out to be useful for the task.

cards.title: Same trick as the perceptron
cards.body: Remember the perceptron learning rule — nudging weights up or down whenever it guessed wrong? Learning a filter works the exact same way. Backpropagation nudges each filter's numbers a little after every mistake, until the values settle on a pattern that actually helps.

cards.title: Discovering an edge detector on its own
cards.body: Train a network on thousands of cat photos, and one of its early learned filters often ends up looking remarkably like a Sobel edge detector — not because anyone programmed that in, but because detecting edges turned out to be genuinely useful, and backpropagation found it through trial and error.

cards.title: Not magic — the same weight update, more data
cards.body: It can feel like the network 'understands' images. It doesn't. It's the exact same weighted-sum-and-adjust-on-error idea from a single perceptron, just applied to grids of filter numbers, repeated across thousands of training examples.

left.text:
Engineer picks the exact numbers
Fixed forever

right.text:
Network starts random
Backprop adjusts numbers from data

exercises.prompt: What does it mean for a filter to be 'learned' rather than hand-crafted?

options.text: Its numeric values are discovered automatically from training data, not chosen by a human
It only works on images that have already been compressed
It skips the sliding-window math that classical filters use
It's identical to a classical filter but renamed

exercises.explain: A learned filter's numbers come from training, not from a person choosing them by hand.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Learning a filter's values uses a completely different process than the perceptron's weight-adjusting learning rule.
exercises.explain: False. It's the same core idea — adjust the numbers based on error — just applied to filter values instead of a single perceptron's weights.

exercises.sentence: Just like a perceptron adjusts its weights when it guesses wrong, a network adjusts its filter numbers using ___.
exercises.explain: Backpropagation is the mechanism that nudges filter values toward ones that reduce error.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly describes how a deep learning model gets its edge-detecting filters?

options.text: It starts with random filter numbers, and backpropagation nudges them toward useful values based on training examples
An engineer manually types in the exact Sobel numbers before every training run
The filters are chosen randomly once and never change during training

exercises.explain: Backpropagation is what turns random starting numbers into useful learned filters over training.

exercises.prompt: A classical Sobel filter and a trained network's learned first-layer filter can end up looking very similar. What's the key difference in how each one got its exact numbers?

options.text: Sobel's numbers were chosen once by a human mathematician; the network's numbers were discovered automatically by backpropagation adjusting them across many training examples
There's no real difference — both are typed in by hand by an engineer
The network's filter numbers are chosen randomly and never adjusted during training
Sobel's numbers come from training data, while the network's numbers are hand-designed

exercises.explain: That's the shift: same sliding-kernel math, different source for the numbers. But that doesn't mean classical filters are useless now — next up, where they still earn their keep.
